answer:
a. Because both age $\left(x_1ight)$ and number of bidders $\left(x_2ight)$ are quantitative variables, we write the complete second-order model as follows:

$$
E(y)= eta_0+ eta_1 x_1+ eta_2 x_2+ eta_3 x_1 x_2+ eta_4\left(x_1ight)^2+ eta_5\left(x_2ight)^2
$$


Note that we have added two quadratic terms, $ eta_4\left(x_1ight)^2$ and $ eta_5\left(x_2ight)^2$, to the interaction model of Example 12.6.
b. We used Minitab to fit the model to the data in Table 12.2. The Minitab output is shown in Figure 12.29. The least squares prediction equation, highlighted on the printout, is

$$
\hat{y}=-332+3.21 x_1+14.8 x_2+1.12 x_1 x_2-.003\left(x_1ight)^2-4.18\left(x_2ight)^2
$$


Note that the estimates of the quadratic terms, $ eta_4$ and $ eta_5$, are both negative, implying downward curvature in both the price-age and price-bidders relationships.
c. To determine whether the quadratic terms are useful for predicting price $(y)$, we want to test

$$
 egin{aligned}
& H_0:  eta_4= eta_5=0 \
& H_8: 	ext { At least one of the parameters, }  eta_4 	ext { and }  eta_5 	ext {, differs from } 0 	ext {. }
\end{aligned}
$$


Because the null hypothesis involves a subset of $ eta$ 's, we will need to perform a nested model $F$-test. The complete model is the complete second-order model of part a. The reduced model is obtained by dropping the quadratic terms out of the complete model. Doing so yields:

$$
	ext { Reduced model: } E(y)= eta_0+ eta_1 x_1+ eta_2 x_2+ eta_3 x_1 x_2
$$


We fit this model in Example 12.6. The Minitab printout for this reduced model is reproduced in Figure 12.30.

To conduct this test, we first obtain the SSE values for the complete and reduced models. These values (highlighted, respectively, on Figures 12.29 and 12.30) are

$$
\mathrm{SSE}_{\mathrm{C}}=192,752 	ext { and } \mathrm{SSE}_{\mathrm{R}}=221,362
$$


Also, $s^2$ for the complete model (highlighted on Figure 12.29) is

$$
\mathrm{s}^2=\mathrm{MSE}_{\mathrm{C}}=7,414
$$


Recall that $n=32$ and that there are $k=5$ terms in the complete model and $g=3$ terms in the reduced model. Therefore, the calculated value of the test statistic is

$$
	ext { Test statistic: } F= rac{\left(\mathrm{SSE}_{\mathrm{R}}-\mathrm{SSE}_{\mathrm{C}}ight) /(k-g)}{\mathrm{MSE}_{\mathrm{C}}}= rac{(221,362-192,752) / 2}{7,414}=1.93
$$


The critical $F$-value for the rejection region is based on $
u_1=(k-g)=2$ numerator df and $
u_2=[n-(k+1)]=26$ denominator df. If we choose $\alpha=.05$, this critical value is $F_{0 \mathrm{~s}}=3.37$ and the rejection region is

Rejection region: $F>3.37$
Because the test statistic, $F=1.93$, falls outside the rejection region, we fail to reject $H_0$. That is, there is insufficient evidence (at $\alpha=.05$ ) to conclude that at least one of the quadratic terms contributes information for the prediction of auction price (y). Consequently, there is no evidence of curvature in either the price-age or pricebidders relationship. It appears that the first-order, interaction (reduced) model is adequate for predicting auction price.

meta_info.caption: Analyzing a Complete Second-Order Model- Auction Price Data
